coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous fluorescence sensing of vitamin B2 and sulfur ions based on fluorescent copper nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huan
Mu, Wencheng
Wang, Siying
Liu, Yuanyuan
Ran, Baocheng
Shi, Lin
Ma, Tianfeng
Lu, Yongchang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the F-CuNPs were synthesized by a modified liquid-phase chemical reduction method. Throughout the preparation process, anhydrous copper sulfate was used as the copper source, and ascorbic acid in the NaOH solution served as the reducing and protective agent. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) may exist between F-CuNPs and vitamin B2 due to the large spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission spectra of F-CuNPs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of vitamin B2. Therefore, the detection of vitamin B2 was designed based on a FRET system between F-CuNPs and vitamin B2. With S2− into the F-CuNPs&VB2 system, the fluorescence intensity of vitamin B2 was quenched, while the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs was almost unchanged. There may be a specific reaction between S2− and vitamin B2. Therefore, the research system can be further used to detect S2− based on ratiometric fluorescent probe. The research findings show that the linear range of vitamin B2 was 0.51 nM–34.64 nM with a detection limit of 0.25 nM (S/N = 3), the linear range of S2− was 0.64 μM–60.00 μM with a detection limit of 0.32 μM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the simultaneous fluorescent sensing system has high sensitivity and selectivity. Therefore, this system was designed and successfully used to detect the content of vitamin B2 and S2− in actual samples to find a new effective method to detect analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: F-CuNPs
FRET
Vitamin B2
S2−
Ratio fluorescence probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of VB2 and S2-
Pretreatment and analysis of actual samples
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of sensing system
Detection of VB2 and S2− in actual samples
Discussion on the mechanism of detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Qinghai Province , China (Grant No. 2019-ZJ-944Q ); the Provincial Thousand Talents Program of High-level Innovation Talent, China ; the Key Laboratory of Protection, Development and Utilization of Tibetan Medicine Resources on Qinghai-Tibet Plateau , State Ethnic Affairs Commission , China; the University-level Planning Project of Qinghai Nationality University of Qinghai Province in China (Grant Nos. 2021XJGH16 , 2022GH11 ); and the Innovation team of Medicinal material resource protection and high value utilization in Qinghai Province (Grant No. 2021XJPI02 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the principle of the sensor system.
F-CuNPs were synthesized at different concentrations of ascorbic acid (A), different temperatures (B), different pH (C), and different reaction time (D).

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of F-CuNPs (inset: F-CuNPs solution color under sunlight (left) and 360 nm UV light (right)); (B) Fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of F-CuNPs at 360 nm; (C) Fluorescence emission spectrum of F-CuNPs at excitation wavelengths of 260 nm, 280 nm, 300 nm, 320 nm, 340 nm, 360 nm, 365 nm, 380 nm and 400 nm; (D) FT-IR of the F-CuNPs and AA. . (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(A) Tem of the F-CuNPs; (B) HR-TEM of the F-CuNPs; (C) TEM of the F-CuNPs&VB2; (D) TEM of the F-CuNPs&VB2&S2−.
(A) XPS spectra of the F-CuNPs; The high-resolution C 1s (B) and Cu 2p (C) peak of the F-CuNPs; (D) EDS spectra of the F-CuNPs; (E) XRD analysis of the synthesized F-CuNPs.
(A) Size distribution potential and (B) ζ-potential distribution of the synthesized F-CuNPs.
Fluorescence intensity of the F-CuNPs at different concentrations of KCl solutions (A), irradiation time with xenon lamps (B), and exposure time (C).

Fluorescence intensity of the F-CuNPs in different buffer solutions (A); different pH (B); (C) different incubation time between F-CuNPs and VB2; (D) different incubation time between F-CuNPs&VB2 and S2−.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs with increasing concentration of VB2; (B) Linear fitting curve of fluorescence intensity at 525 nm with increasing concentration of VB2; (C) Fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs&VB2 system with increasing concentration of S2−; (D) Linear fitting curve of the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 440 nm to fluorescence intensity at 525 nm with increasing concentration of S2−.
Effect of different interfering substances on FL intensity of the F-CuNPs (A) and F-CuNPs&VB2 solution (B).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-CuNPs (black line), F-CuNPs&VB2 (red line) and F-CuNPs&VB2&S2− (blue line); (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of VB2 (red line) and fluorescence emission spectra of F-CuNPs (blue line); (C) Fluorescence lifetime of F-CuNPs with the addition of VB2; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of VB2, S2- and their mixtures. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of different methods for VB2 sensing.
Comparison of different methods for S2− sensing.
Determination of VB2 by the F-CuNPs-based simultaneous fluorescence sensor.
Determination of S2− by the F-CuNPs-based simultaneous fluorescence sensor.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous fluorescence sensing of vitamin B2 and sulfur ions based on fluorescent copper nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Phytochemistry Key Laboratory of Tibetan Plateau of Qinghai Province, College of Pharmacy, Qinghai Nationalities University, Xining, 810007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Phytochemistry Key Laboratory of Tibetan Plateau of Qinghai Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Phytochemistry Key Laboratory of Tibetan Plateau of Qinghai Province; College of Pharmacy, Qinghai Nationalities University, Xining 810007, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: 96602 Military Hospital of Chinese People's Liberation Army, Kunming, 650000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: 96602 Military Hospital of Chinese People's Liberation Army

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 96602 Military Hospital of Chinese People's Liberation Army, Kunming 650000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yinchuan Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Yinchuan, 750004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yinchuan Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yinchuan Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Yinchuan 750004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. No. 3, Bayi Middle Road, Xining, Qinghai, 810007, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Huan Wang and Wencheng Mu are joint first authors.
These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the F-CuNPs were synthesized by a modified liquid-phase chemical reduction method. Throughout the preparation process, anhydrous copper sulfate was used as the copper source, and ascorbic acid in the NaOH solution served as the reducing and protective agent. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) may exist between F-CuNPs and vitamin B2 due to the large spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission spectra of F-CuNPs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of vitamin B2. Therefore, the detection of vitamin B2 was designed based on a FRET system between F-CuNPs and vitamin B2. With S2− into the F-CuNPs&VB2 system, the fluorescence intensity of vitamin B2 was quenched, while the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs was almost unchanged. There may be a specific reaction between S2− and vitamin B2. Therefore, the research system can be further used to detect S2− based on ratiometric fluorescent probe. The research findings show that the linear range of vitamin B2 was 0.51 nM–34.64 nM with a detection limit of 0.25 nM (S/N = 3), the linear range of S2− was 0.64 μM–60.00 μM with a detection limit of 0.32 μM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the simultaneous fluorescent sensing system has high sensitivity and selectivity. Therefore, this system was designed and successfully used to detect the content of vitamin B2 and S2− in actual samples to find a new effective method to detect analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my data at the Attach Flie step

ce:section.ce:para: It is commonly regarded that VB2 (Vitamin B2) plays a significant role in the metabolism of proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates and is generally present in fruits, vegetables, and medications. It participated in the metabolism of protein, fat and carbohydrate. If VB2 is absorbed too much in the body, it will cause oxidative damage to tissues and DNA owing to its highly effective photosensitivity [1]. However, its absence from the diet might result in stomatitis, skin problems, eye diseases, and other illnesses [2]. In previous experiments, our study team has founded that S2− has a special interaction with the VB2 molecular structure [3]. However, sulfur ions (S2−), as a traditional pollutant, has recently received attention. In addition, HS− and H2S produced by S2− protonation have shown to be more toxic and harmful than sulfide ions [4–6]. Research has shown that the dynamic imbalance of S2− in organisms, which may contribute to diseases including Down syndrome, diabetes, and Alzheimer's [7–9]. Therefore, it is an urgent research goal to develop a rapid, sensitive and accurate method for simultaneous detection of VB2 and S2−.

Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) refers to the photophysical process of non-radiative energy transfer between the fluorescent donor group and acceptor group [10]. At least two conditions are required for the FRET phenomenon. Firstly, the emission spectrum of the fluorescent donor must overlap by more than 30% with the absorption spectrum of the fluorescent acceptor [11]. Secondly, the distance between the fluorescent donor and fluorescent acceptor is less than 10 nm [12]. Only the above conditions are met, the energy of the fluorescent donor is absorbed by the neighboring fluorescent acceptor [13]. FRET technology has been extensively applied in biosensors recently, including accurate detection of metal ions [14], DNA [15], glucose [16], and other molecules. This is due to its benefits of simple equipment, fewer samples needed, high sensitivity, and ease of analysis [17].

Nanoparticles (NPs) have found widespread usage in disciplines including environmental monitoring [18] and biomedical molecular detection [19] because of their ability to alter the chromatic, fluorescent, and electrochemical characteristics of substances which depending on the interactions within the material [20]. Metal nanoparticles (NPs) mainly include gold nanoparticles, silver nanoparticles and copper nanoparticles (CuNPs). In these nanoparticles, CuNPs should be given preference for application over the others since they are non-toxic, economical, and abundant in earth resources [21]. Zeenat Arif et al. reported the use of copper chloride as a precursor, sodium formaldehyde sulfoxylate as a reducing agent, oleic acid as a coating agent to prevent agglomeration, and surfactants like polyvinyl alcohol to prevent oxide formation in the reduction of copper precursors from Cun + to Cu0 [22]. Wang et al. designed a bifunctional polypeptide (CCYGGPKKKRKVG), which was employed as a reducing agent and stabilizer in the effective synthesis of CuNCs [23]. Li et al. achieved S2−specific detection by using S2− to induce aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement of CuNCs, with a detection limit of 42 nM [24]. Lin et al. used a hydrothermal approach to synthesis PVP-CuNCs and built a dual-emission ratio fluorescent probe for the selective detection of rutin and picric acid [25]. Wang et al. successfully synthesized red luminescent F-CuNPs that eliminate biological background interference using a bovine albumin serum protein template (BSA) via a biomineralization route. Subsequently, a fluorescent test strip was fabricated using for the highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ in blood based on the F-CuNPs [26]. In summary the synthetic methods of CuNPs especially consist of liquid phase chemical reduction [27], thermal decomposition [28], polyol method [29], electrodeposition [30], microemulsion method [31], and so forth. Among these methods, the liquid phase chemical reduction method is widely used due to its simplicity in operation. This thesis will be devoted to synthesizing blue fluorescent copper nanoparticles with high quantum yields by modified liquid-phase chemical reduction method.

In this paper, we first synthesized blue fluorescent copper nanoparticles (F-CuNPs) using copper sulfate served as the copper source, while ascorbic acid (AA) in the NaOH solution served as the reducing and protecting agent. Then, a simultaneous fluorescence sensor was constructed based on the F-CuNPs fluorescent probe, which working principle is shown in Fig. 1 . When the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, the prepared F-CuNPs were found to have a distinct fluorescence emission peak at 440 nm. Excitingly, the fluorescence emission peak moved from 440 nm to 525 nm when VB2 was added to the F-CuNPs solution, most likely as a result of FRET. When S2− was added to the F-CuNPs&VB2 sensing system, the fluorescence intensity of VB2 at 525 nm was quenched, while the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs at 440 nm was essentially unchanged, suggesting that the specific reaction between VB2 and S2− is stronger than the covalent interaction between S2− and F-CuNPs. Compared with other detection methods, the simultaneous fluorescence sensor we produced has the advantages of high sensitivity and good selectivity and has been successfully applied to the detection of VB2 and S2− in actual samples.
Materials and instruments have been provided in the Supplementary Material.

F-CuNPs was synthesized by the chemical reduction method of the classical liquid-phase. The main experimental methods are as follows: 35.0 mg CuSO4·5H2O and 2 mL 0.5 mol•L−1 NaOH solution were respectively added with 18 mL ultrapure water. The mixture was stirred for 2 min at room temperature and then transferred to a rotary evaporation flask containing 20 mL 0.1 mol•L−1 of AA solution. At this moment, the mixture solution's color was turned colorless into dark pink. After the above mixture solution was further rotated for 10 min at 80 °C, an appropriate amount of 0.5 mol•L−1 NaOH solution was added to adjust the pH at 7, and the mixture was continued to rotate for 10 h. The solution's color eventually changed from dark pink to greenish brown without any precipitate. The F-CuNPs were successfully prepared and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C in the dark.

The quantum yield of F-CuNPs was determined by the relative method using quinine sulfate as standard [32]. The quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4) and F-CuNPs solution was prepared. Then their absorbance was recorded. To minimize reabsorption effects, The measurement of absorbance was kept under 0.1 in the 1 cm cuvette. After that, the fluorescence intensity of the same solutions was recorded upon excitation at 360 nm, and quantum yield was calculated using the following equation.
Q
Y s
= Q
Y
s t
η
s 2
η
s t
2
I s
A s
A
s t
I
s t
Where QY is the quantum yield;
η
is the refractive index of the solvent; s represents the sample; st represents the standard; I is the fluorescence intensity, and A is the absorbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of VB2 and S2-
ce:section.ce:para: F-CuNPs were used as fluorescent initiation probes in VB2 and S2− sensing research. The experiment was conducted at 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution the (PBS) with a pH of 6 in a quartz cell (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) at room temperature. The specific process is explained as follows: 15 μL of the prepared F-CuNPs solution and 1.8 mL 0.1 M PBS were mixed in the quartz cell. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of maximum fluorescence emission was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm when different concentrations of VB2 solution were continuously dropped into the above mixture and incubation for 2.5 min. Appropriate amounts of actual samples of VB2 tablet solution and orange juice solution were added to the F-CuNPs probe for examination. In addition, stock solutions of different concentrations S2− were continuously dropped into the solution of the F-CuNPs&VB2 system, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity was measured after 30 min of incubation. Appropriate amounts of actual samples of rabbit serum solution and Coca-Cola solution were added to the F-CuNPs&VB2 ratio meter sensing system for examination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Take VB2 2 tablets, ground, precisely weighed the quantity of fine powder (approximately equivalent to VB2 5.0 mg), placed in a 100 mL volumetric flask, adequate ultrasonic (200 W, 40 kHz) to dissolve, and diluted to scale, shaken, filtered, take the subsequent filtrate, that is VB2 (0.05 g/L) solution.

The rabbit's ear was used to collect 1 mL of blood, which was centrifuged since being kept at room temperature for 4 h and refrigerated for 12 h. In an ultraclean hood, the top layer of serum was removed by centrifuging it for 15 min at 4000 rpm. Following the abovementioned preparation procedure, the serum was collected, after aseptic processing and then refrigerated for analysis.

Centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min with 5 mL of orange juice or Coca-Cola solution, the supernatant was then diluted with ultrapure water to fill a 50 mL colorimetric tube. The SPE-C18 solid phase extraction column was used to extract the abovementioned diluent. 5 mL of the filtrate was collected after the initial 1 mL of liquid was discarded. The filtrate was utilized as the stock solution for the analysis after being passed through a nylon 0.22 mm microporous membrane.

To improve the fluorescence sensing performance of F-CuNPs, the synthesis conditions of F-CuNPs were optimized using the controlled variable method. In this paper, the concentration of AA, reaction temperature, reaction time, reaction pH in the reaction were investigated in Fig. 2 . As revealed in Fig. 2A, the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized F-CuNPs showed an increasing trend followed by a decreasing trend with the concentration of AA gradually increased from 0.04 M to 0.14 M. It showed the maximum fluorescence intensity at 0.1 M. Therefore, 0.1 M was selected as the optimal concentration of ascorbic acid. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs at different temperatures was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 2B. The result of research showed that F-CuNPs demonstrated the maximum fluorescence intensity at 80 °C when the reaction temperatures were 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C, 70 °C, 80 °C and 90 °C, respectively. In addition, the reaction pH also was optimized in Fig. 2C. The findings showed that the corresponding fluorescence intensity was the strongest at 7 when pH were 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, respectively, which further suggesting that NaOH would affect the synthesis of F-CuNPs with AA concentration. This means that AA has the strongest reducibility under neutral conditions, and its activity is reduced by hydrolysis to diketogulloxylic acid under alkaline or acidic conditions. Finally, the effect of reaction time to the fluorescence properties of F-CuNPs also plays a very important role. It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized F-CuNPs was the largest at 10 h in Fig. 2D when reaction time were 3 h, 4 h, 5 h, 6 h, 7 h, 8 h, 9 h, 10 h, 11 h, respectively. Therefore, the above optimal reaction conditions were adopted in the subsequent synthesis of F-CuNPs. Excitingly, the prepared F-CuNPs quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be as high as 34.5%.

In order to prove the successful preparation of F-CuNPs, the optical properties of the synthesized F-CuNPs were investigated by UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence spectra, and fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) in Fig. 3 . The prepared copper nanoparticles were studied by the UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra in Fig. 3A. It could be seen from the UV–vis absorption spectra (blue line) that the distinct UV absorption at 295 nm was the characteristic absorption peak of F-CuNPs. The fluorescence emission spectra (red line) of F-CuNPs showed the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength was obtained at 440 nm under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The inner illustration displayed the F-CuNPs solution appearing brown under sunlight (left), and blue fluorescence was irradiated with the UV lamp at 360 nm (right). The fluorescence excitation spectrum (purple wire) and emission spectrum (orange line) at 360 nm excitation wavelength of F-CuNPs were illustrated in Fig. 3B. Fluorescence emission spectra indicated that the synthesized F-CuNPs had narrow and symmetric emission bands, which further confirming their homogeneity and monodispersity. The excitation-dependent emission properties of the prepared F-CuNPs is further investigated in Fig. 3C. As other fluorescent material, the maximum fluorescence emissions wavelength of F-CuNPs gradually shifted from 420 to 440 nm when the excitation wavelength changed from 260 to 360 nm.

The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was further carried out to verify whether the proposed method could successfully synthesize F-CuNPs as shown in Fig. 3D. It could be seen from the FT-IR of AA (red line) that 1763 cm−1 and 1680 cm−1 were respectively the stretching vibration absorption peak of CO and CC; 1325 cm−1 was the asymmetric stretching vibration absorption peak of C–O–C; 1068 cm−1 was the bending vibration absorption peak of –OH; and 1031 cm−1 was symmetric stretching vibration absorption peak of C–O–C. Moreover, the FT-IR of F-CuNPs was shown in the blue line of Fig. 3D. 3451 cm−1, 1635 cm−1 and 1363 cm−1 were the stretching vibration absorption peak of O–H, CC, and CC–OH, respectively. These findings indicated that ascorbic acid as a coating agent was present on the surface of F-CuNPs, which meaning F-CuNPs were successfully synthesized.

In order to further study the morphological characteristics of the prepared F-CuNPs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of F-CuNPs, F-CuNPs&VB2 and F-CuNPs&VB2&S2− were reserved in Fig. 4 . The findings suggested that the prepared F-CuNPs had an excellent spherical structure and uniform dispersion by testing the particle size distribution of more than 50 nanodots randomly selected in Fig. 4A. The inset further displayed particle size distribution of the prepared F-CuNPs in the range of 8–13 nm, with an average size of approximately 10 nm. Fig. 4B revealed individual nano-particle image by the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The lattice spacing of synthesized F-CuNPs was 1.81 nm. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the further introduction of VB2 led to severe aggregation of the F-CuNPs, suggesting that this aggregation led to changes in the fluorescence emission peaks of F-CuNPs. In Fig. 4D, the degree of dispersion of F-CuNPs became more dispersed compared with Fig. 4C when S2− was further introduced into F-CuNPs&VB2 system. This dispersion results further indicated that the specific reaction between S2− and VB2 led to a significant decrease in the interaction between VB2 and F-CuNPs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to understand the valence state of the element and composition of F-CuNPs as shown in Fig. 5 (A-C). Fig. 5A results showed that the elemental composition of F-CuNPs was relatively complete, mainly contained carbon, oxygen and copper. The high-resolution XPS characterization of C 1s showed three peaks at 284.7, 286.4, and 288.3 eV in Fig. 5B, which belonged to C–C, C–O and CO, respectively [33]. What is more, the high-resolution XPS characterization of Cu 2p was illustrated in Fig. 5C. There were two prominent peaks at 932.5 and 952.7 eV, which could be assigned to the energy levels of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 electrons of Cu(0), respectively. This meant that AA reduced Cu2+ to CuNPs. It is noteworthy that Cu(0) is very unstable in the air and can be easily oxidized to Cu(Ⅰ). Therefore, the 2p2/3 energy level of Cu(0) detected in the CuNPs may be attributed to the formation of Cu(Ⅰ). In particular, the difference of 0.1 eV between Cu(0) and Cu(Ⅰ) in the 2p2/3 energy level makes it difficult to distinguish them. Therefore, the valence state of Cu in the synthesized F-CuNPs appears between Cu(0) and Cu(I). In addition, the elemental composition of the synthesized F-CuNPs was further investigated by Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) analysis. As shown in Fig. 5D, signals belonging to the element copper (0.94 keV, CuLα; 8.0 keV, CuKα; 8.9 keV, CuKβ) endorsed the successful synthesis of F-CuNPs [34]. The weak signals of C and O appear together with strong metallic copper peaks, probably due to the presence of biomolecules bound to the surface of F-CuNPs. The crystallinity of the synthesized F-CuNPs has been investigated via X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD). XRD analysis of the prepared F-CuNPs indicated that only metallic copper was obtained, which was confirmed by the diffraction peak of the only existing cubic phase. (Fm3m, space group number 225, Fig. 5E). Moreover, the lattice parameter was calculated to be 3.609 Å based on the powder diffraction pattern, which was the same as the previously reported literature [35].

To confirm the monodispersity and homogeneity of the synthesized F-CuNPs, the size distribution of the F-CuNPs were studied using dynamic light scattering (DLS). The average size of the F-CuNPs recorded by DLS is about 133.2 nm (Fig. 6 A). The size disparity between DLS and TEM is due to the different principles involved in these techniques. The DLS readings are measured in solution. Therefore, the readings always show a higher hydrodynamic size (about 10 times) than the particles size in the dry state measured by TEM [36]. The polydispersity index (PDI) is close to 0.495 nm, indicating that the synthesized F-CuNPs have the mono dispersive nature. The ζ-potential defines the stability of the colloid. Secondly, it also is a standard measure of the surface charge of the particles. A zeta potential of at least ±30 mV is required if the stability of the NP suspension is to be maintained by electrostatic repulsion [37]. In this study, the ζ-potential of F-CuNPs was measured by DLS to be +33.7 mV (Fig. 6B), suggesting that the surface charge of F-CuNPs mitigates particle aggregation and imparts stability.

The luminescence properties of F-CuNPs were explored as influenced by salt concentration, light exposure time, and storage time. Fig. 7 A showed that the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs remained essentially constant when they were in 0.1 M–1.0 M KCl solutions. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs remained almost unchanged after being continuously irradiated by the xenon lamp for 80 min (Fig. 7B). Surprisingly, the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs had little change even after being stored at 4 °C for 50 days (Fig. 7C). It is exciting that the synthesized F-CuNPs have good salt tolerance, light tolerance, and placement stability.

It is well known that the fluorescence intensity of fluorophores is influenced by the type of buffer solution, the pH of the buffer solution, and the reaction time. Therefore, five buffer solutions were selected as PBS, Borate-Buffer, Bertrand-Robinson, Borate-KCl-NaOH, and Tris-HCl to examine the effect on the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs at the excitation wavelength of 440 nm. It was not difficult to see in Fig. 8 A that the corresponding fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs was highest in PBS buffer solutions among all the buffer solutions with pH = 7. Therefore, PBS was chosen as the best buffer solution system in the subsequent experiments. This relationship between the different pH of the 0.1 M PBS buffer solution and the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs at 440 nm maximum fluorescence emission wavelength was shown in Fig. 8B. It was obvious that the maximum fluorescence intensity of the F-CuNPs was about 777 a. u. when the pH value was 6. So the optimal pH value of PBS buffer solution was 6. Meanwhile, the reaction time between the fluorophores and the detector was further investigated, as shown in Fig. 8C. These results suggested that the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was almost unchanged when the reaction time reached 2.5 min when a specific concentration of VB2 was added to the F-CuNPs solution. Therefore, 2.5 min was selected as the optimal reaction time for the F-CuNPs&VB2 sensing system. The reaction time of S2− and F-CuNPs&VB2 sensing system was investigated in Fig. 8D. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the proposed sensing system at 525 nm decreased continuously with the increase of the reaction time, but it has little changed when 30 min was reached. Therefore, 30 min was selected as the optimal reaction time of the sensing system in the following experiment.

The fluorescence intensity of the F-CuNPs decreased and a new fluorescence peak was generated at 525 nm when different concentrations of VB2 were introduced into the precursor fluorophores. The effect of different concentrations of VB2 on the F-CuNPs fluorescence intensity was examined in Fig. 9 A. It was observed that there was a gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity at 525 nm and a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 400 nm with increasing VB2 concentration. Therefore, the fluorescence signal peak of VB2 at 525 nm was used as a reference peak to fit the linear relationship in Fig. 9B. The linear fitting curve equation applied was Y = 23.6534 [VB2]+127.2018 (R
2 = 0.9998), the linear range of VB2 detection was 0.51 nM–34.64 nM, and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 0.25 nM by 3δ/k (δ: the blank standard deviation; k: the slope of the linear fitting curve equation). Another meaningful finding was that S2− could burst the fluorescence intensity of VB2 at 525 nm, while it had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs at 440 nm with the increase of S2− concentration, as shown in Fig. 9C. Therefore, the S2− sensor can be constructed based on ratio meter fluorescent probe of F-CuNPs&VB2. Fig. 9D showed the linear fitting curve by the relationship between the calculated fluorescence intensity ratio (FL440/FL525) and the concentration of S2−. The linear equation of the S2− sensor is Y = 0.0044 [S2−]+0.6346 (R
2 = 0.9986) with the linear ranges of 0.64 μM–60.00 μM, and the LOD is as low as 0.32 μM. Table 1 and Table 2 were respectively the result of contrast of the simultaneous sensing platform constructed in this study and other reported detection systems. Satisfyingly, the proposed sensing system has higher accuracy and sensitivity than other detection systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the sensor system determines whether the sensor can be applied to detect real samples. Hence, the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the advocated recognition system was investigated, as shown in Fig. 10 A. It was shown that the fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs at 440 nm was significantly reduced when a specific concentration of VB2 was added to the F-CuNPs solution, where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of F-CuNPs in the absence and presence of VB2. Unsurprisingly, the extent of fluorescence burst of F-CuNPs was insignificant when 100 times VB2 concentration of interfering substances were added to F-CuNPs solution. The interfering substances mainly included Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 2−, Glucose, Lactose, Sucrose, Alanine, Arginine, Methionine, Vitamin B1, Vitamin B3, Vitamin B6, Vitamin B9, Vitamin B12, and Vitamin C. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of the solution at 525 nm decreased significantly in Fig. 10B when a specific concentration of S2− was added to the F-CuNPs&VB2 solution, where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of VB2 in the absence and presence of S2−. And the fluorescence intensity of the selected interfering substances at 100 times the concentration of S2− had little change significantly compared to that of the F-CuNPs&VB2/S2− solution, where the interfering substances included Citrate, Tartarate, Lysine, Phenylalanine, Methionine, Cysteine, GSH, Glucose, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO2 − and NO3 −. The simultaneous fluorescence sensor designed in the study shows good selectivity and anti-interference ability for the detection of VB2 and S2− in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of VB2 and S2− in actual samples
Discussion on the mechanism of detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was weakened based on the specific interaction between F-CuNPs and VB2 when VB2 was added to the F-CuNPs probe solution, and a new fluorescence emission peak at 525 nm was appeared, as shown in Fig. 11 A. Fluorescence quenching processes can be classified as internal filtration effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET), etc., of which IFE and FRET are common. In order to investigate the interaction mechanism of them, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of VB2 (red line) and the fluorescence emission spectrum of F-CuNPs (blue line) were studied in this paper, as shown in Fig. 11B. The experiment results showed that there was a significant overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum and the fluorescence emission spectrum. Therefore, FRET was likely to exist between F-CuNPs (donor) and VB2 (acceptor). To further prove that FRET, rather than the internal filtration effect (IEF), occurred between F-CuNPs and VB2, the fluorescence lifetimes of F-CuNPs before and after the addition of VB2 were measured in Fig. 11C. With the addition of VB2, the average fluorescence lifetime of F-CuNPs decreased from 6.798 ns to 5.899 ns? Since the IFE process did not change the fluorescence lifetime of F-CuNPs, the possibility of IFE occurring between F-CuNPs and VB2 was excluded. Therefore, it was further proved that the reduction of fluorescence intensity of F-CuNPs and the generation of VB2 fluorescence peak were based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Moreover, it was found that S2− could quenched the fluorescence intensity at 525 nm, while it had subtle changed for the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm in Fig. 11A. Therefore, the mechanism of the above phenomenon was further studied by UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 11D. It can be seen that the characteristic peak of VB2 at 370 nm produces blue-shifted when S2− is introduced into the VB2 solution, which may be due to the specific reaction between S2− and the amide group of VB2 molecular structure [3]. The interaction could cause S2− to disrupt the ring structure of the VB2 and reduce the unsaturation of the VB2 molecular structures, which resulting blue-shifts of the UV absorption spectrum at 370 nm. Therefore, based on this mechanism, a ratio meter sensing system was constructed.

In summary, we successfully synthesized blue fluorescent copper nanoparticles by a modified liquid-phase chemical reduction method and optimized the synthesis conditions, laying the foundation for analyzing biological systems. Meanwhile, the F-CuNPs were further characterized by various spectra. After stability tests on F-CuNPs, it was found that the synthesized F-CuNPs had good salt tolerance, light tolerance, and placement stability. More importantly, the simultaneous fluorescence sensor based on F-CuNPs was successfully constructed. In addition, the sensing conditions of the simultaneous fluorescence sensor were optimized, and the anti-interference capability of this sensing system was investigated. Finally, the sensor was effectively evaluated for the quantification of VB2 and S2− in actual samples by the spiked recovery method. Satisfyingly, compared with some methods, the simultaneous fluorescence sensing technology has the advantages of fast response, high sensitivity, and technical simplicity. Therefore, the simultaneous fluorescence sensor has excellent potential for future practice.

Huan Wang: Reviewing, Validation. Wencheng Mu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing-Original draft. Siying Wang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing-Reviewing and Editing, Resource. Yuanyuan Liu: Data curation. Baocheng Ran: Investigation. Lin Shi: Reviewing. Tianfeng Ma: Supervision. Yongchang Lu: Formal analysis, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Qinghai Province, China (Grant No.2019-ZJ-944Q); the Provincial Thousand Talents Program of High-level Innovation Talent, China; the Key Laboratory of Protection, Development and Utilization of Tibetan Medicine Resources on Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, China; the University-level Planning Project of Qinghai Nationality University of Qinghai Province in China (Grant Nos.2021XJGH16, 2022GH11); and the Innovation team of Medicinal material resource protection and high value utilization in Qinghai Province (Grant No.2021XJPI02).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124267.
